query: HeimWall: Catch secrets before they leak into Cursor & Claude complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Stop Leaking Secrets to Claude Code: A Practical Security ...
results.content:
Three Leak Paths Into Conversation Context

1. Direct file reads. You ask Claude to refactor your config loader. It opens `.env` to understand the variable names, and the values come along for the ride. This is the textbook leak and the easiest one to block.
2. Runtime output capture. Claude runs your test suite or boots your dev server. A failing request logs the full `Authorization: Bearer sk-live-...` header. A database error dumps the connection string with the password. The `.env` file was never opened, but the secrets are now in context anyway, courtesy of your own application's logs.
3. Search and grep results. Claude runs grep for a function name. The match happens to be next to a hardcoded API key in a forgotten config file. The grep output includes surrounding lines. [...] Per Anthropic's docs, sandbox `denyRead` paths are "merged with any paths from `Edit(...)` and `Read(...)` permission rules", so your existing deny list extends naturally into the sandbox boundary. With sandboxing on, `cat .env` from within bash hits the same wall as the Read tool would.

Linux and WSL2 require `bubblewrap` and `socat` to be installed first:

```
# Ubuntu/Debian sudo apt-get install bubblewrap socat # Fedora sudo dnf install bubblewrap socat
```

macOS works out of the box.

## Blocking Runtime Leaks at the Application Layer

Even with sandboxing, your application can still print secrets to stdout that Claude then captures as tool output. The simplest fix is to make sure the secrets the app sees during dev and test are not the real ones. [...] Why this works

If the only credentials your dev process ever sees are dummies, then the worst case for path 2 is that a dummy ends up in a transcript. Production keys never enter the loop because they were never on disk.

## Catching Mistakes at Commit Time

Pre-commit hooks will not stop Claude from reading a secret, but they will stop you from pushing it to GitHub. Drop this in `.git/hooks/pre-commit`:

results.title: The Agent Secrets Pattern: Giving AI Access Without Git Exposure | Mario Giancini
results.content:
In December 2025, researchers discovered over 30 vulnerabilities across major AI coding tools including Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, and Windsurf—resulting in 24 CVEs. One critical vulnerability (CVE-2025-55284) allowed attackers to exfiltrate secrets via DNS requests through prompt injection.

Your app's `.env` might contain:

 Database connection strings
 Payment processor keys
 Admin API tokens

Your agent doesn't need any of that to log into LinkedIn and scrape a profile. And if a vulnerability allows context leakage, you want to minimize what's exposed.

By separating agent credentials, you: [...] Additional protection: In your VSCode/Cursor settings, you can prevent `.env` files from being opened at all, which also blocks composer and chat access:

```
{"files.exclude":{"/.env": true true,"/.env.": true true}}
```

## Defense in Depth: PreToolUse Hooks

Given the reported reliability issues with deny rules, I now recommend adding a PreToolUse hook as a backup protection layer. Hooks intercept tool calls before execution and can block them based on custom logic.

Add this to your `.claude/settings.local.json`: [...] ## The Pattern: A Dedicated `.agent-secrets` File

```
# Template (committed)# Your credentials (gitignored)# Blocks .agent-secrets from git# Allows .agent-secrets for Cursor # Grants explicit read access for Claude Code
```

The key insight: you need to explicitly deny access to sensitive files, not just rely on `.gitignore`.

Here's what I learned the hard way: Claude Code doesn't fully respect `.gitignore` for file reading—it may skip gitignored files in search operations, but it can still read them directly. And Cursor's ignore functionality is officially described as "best effort" rather than guaranteed. You need layered protection.

## Configuring Claude Code

results.title: Are AI Coding Tools Leaking Your Secrets? Probably Not
results.content:
Check your setup. Cursor has sensible defaults. Does your AI editor? Are you using them?
   Rotate API keys regularly. It’s password hygiene for your systems.
   Use secret managers like Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Doppler.
   Never hardcode secrets directly into your codebase.
   Use`.env.example` to show config variables without exposing real values.
   Treat your`.env`like your toothbrush: personal, replaceable, and definitely not shared.

## 💡 You’re Probably Fine (But Rotate Your Keys Anyway)

AI tools like Cursor are your helpful assistant — not your nosy neighbor. They index your code, not your secrets. They transmit encrypted fingerprints, not your files.

The biggest risk isn’t the tool. It’s forgetting that good security hygiene still matters. [...] Developers typically store them in `.env` files on their machines so that they don’t get permanently & visibly written into code.

A `.env` file looks something like this:

STRIPE_API_KEY=sk_live_1234567890

DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@db.example.com
Those values are not meant to be public, and the ones on developer machines are (hopefully) NOT the same ones used in production.

Accidentally committing a `.env` file to GitHub or is like posting your house alarm code on Twitter.

That’s why `.env` files should be ignored by version control — and why modern AI tools like Cursor should take extra steps to avoid touching them at all.

## 🧠 How AI Tools Actually “Read” Your Code [...] ## 🧩 How Cursor Handles This Behind the Scenes

Here’s the reassuring part: Cursor was designed with this concern in mind.

   Files are indexed locally, on your computer — not on Cursor’s servers.
   The index is encrypted and stored securely on your machine.
   When you make a request, Cursor sends only the relevant, vectorized pieces of that index to the model — not your full codebase.
   Those vectors are temporary, deleted once the response is complete.
   `.env` files and secret files are excludedby default.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image 3

Cursor documentation showing files that are ignored by default

I even asked Cursor directly if it could see my `.env` file. The AI responded:

results.title: Tried claude code.  Hate it.
results.content:
## Top Comments
- Cursor + plan mode seems substantially better than Claude code to me. I also hate all the vibe coded slop with no real thought given. It makes a mockery of the profession and likely will fuck us all on jobs and eventually in key products people use.
- Cursor is my day to day driver. I'm a designer at heart and can't imagine not having the ability to click on DOM elements to make direct changes. I've never understood the CC love from everyone
- honestly the hour you spend in ask mode before touching code is doing more work than the agent itself. the planning step is the actual leverage and most people skip it entirely (then complain the tool "hallucinates"). cursor just happens to make that step less painful to do [...] - The only thing that makes Claude popular is the amount of ad spend they put into it.
- What models do you use in cursor? Aside from composer I feel like the speed should be similar?
- I've been on Gemini a few months and like it was with jules extension. Minimax is also quite great.
- You can install claude code vscode extension. Both tools are basically the same.
